11: ki raq-Og melekh-haBashan nish'ar miyeter haRefa'im hineh arso arso-barzel (kî raq-ʿÔg melek-habbāšān nišʾar miyyeter hārĕpāʾîm hinnēh ʿarśô ʿereś barzel): 'For only Og the king of Bashan was left of the remnant of the Rephaim. Behold, his bed was a bed of iron.' The Rĕpāʾîm are a category of pre-Israelite inhabitants of Canaan associated with unusual size and formidable strength (Genesis 14:5; Deuteronomy 15:20; Deuteronomy 2:10-11, 20-21). Og's iron bed (ʿereś barzel) — nine cubits long (approximately 13.5 feet) and four cubits wide — was displayed at Rabbah of the Ammonites as a trophy confirming the tradition of his extraordinary stature. The defeat of Og, like that of Sihon (Deuteronomy 2), demonstrates that YHWH's guarantee of victory over the giant clans was not empty: the most formidable pre-Israelite ruler east of the Jordan was given into Israel's hand.
22: lo-tira'u otam ki YHWH Eloheichem hu-hanilcham lachem (lōʾ tîrĕʾû mēhem kî Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkem hû hananicḥlām lākem): 'You shall not fear them, for it is the LORD your God who fights for you.' The divine warrior formula — YHWH himself as Israel's fighter — is the theological summary of Israel's entire military history east of the Jordan. Hu-hanilcham lachem (he is the one who fights for you): the participial form stresses YHWH's ongoing fighting posture, not a one-time act. This formula recurs throughout the conquest narrative (Joshua 10:42; Deuteronomy 23:3,10) and becomes the basis of Israel's liturgical confidence in YHWH as the warrior who goes before his people (Exodus 14:14; Ps 18).
26: vayit'aber YHWH bi lema'ankhem velo-shama elai vayomer YHWH elai rav-lach al-tosef daber elai od badavar hazeh (wayyitʿabbēr Yhwh bî lĕmaʿankhem wĕlōʾ šāmaʿ ʾēlay wayyōʾmer Yhwh ʾēlay rab-lāk ʾal-tôsep̄ dabbēr ʾēlay ʿôd baddābār hazzeh): 'But the LORD was angry with me on your account and would not listen to me. And the LORD said to me, 'Enough from you; do not speak to me of this matter again.'' Wayyitʿabbēr YHWH bî — YHWH crossed over into anger against Moses on Israel's account (lĕmaʿankhem). The verb ʿābar in the Hithpael means to become intensely angry. Moses's intercessory prayer for permission to cross the Jordan (Deuteronomy 3:23-25) was denied with finality: rab-lāk (enough for you). Moses is forbidden from the land not because he is unloved (YHWH's tenderness in Deuteronomy 3:27 is evident) but because his striking of the rock (Numbers 20:7-12) had disqualified him and because he bore the consequences of Israel's corporate sin as their representative leader.
1: vanna'fen vannal min-haderech el-haBashan: 'Then we turned and went up the road to Bashan.' The narrator's first person plural (nafen, naal) is characteristic of Deuteronomy's retelling style — Moses speaks as one who shared the entire journey. Bashan, east of the Sea of Galilee and north of Gilead, was famous for its rich pastureland, oaks (Isaiah 2:13; Ezekiel 27:6), and cattle (Psalms 22:12; Amos 4:1). Its king Og was the last of the Rephaim giant clans; the battle to follow is therefore the climax of the Transjordanian conquest.
2: lo-tira oto ki veyad-cha atatenu oto vekhol-amo ve'et arzo: 'Do not fear him, for into your hand I give him and all his people and his land.' The divine assurance formula — lo-tira (do not fear) + natan beyad (given into your hand) — is the standard holy-war speech act that authorizes and enables the conquest. YHWH's word is the decisive factor; Og's military superiority is irrelevant because YHWH's prior commitment determines the outcome. The same formula drives Joshua's campaigns (Joshua 6:2; Deuteronomy 8:1; Deuteronomy 10:8; Deuteronomy 11:6) and becomes a template for all subsequent assurances of divine provision against impossible odds.
3: vayten YHWH Eloheinu beyadeinu gam et-Og melekh-haBashan: 'So the LORD our God gave into our hands Og also, king of Bashan, and all his people.' The particle gam (also, even) links the defeat of Og with that of Sihon (Deuteronomy 2), creating a paired pattern: two giant kings, two divine victories, one consistent pattern of YHWH's faithfulness. Lo-hishir lo sarid (there was no survivor) — the totality of defeat confirms the herem mandate and demonstrates that YHWH's promise was fully realized.
4: vaniqqach et-kol-arav baat hahi lo hayta qiryah asher lo-laqahnu me'itam: 'We captured all his cities — there was not a city we did not take from them.' Shishim ir (sixty cities) — the number reinforces the magnitude of the victory. The entire region of Argob (kol-hevel Argov) formed a well-defined district of Bashan. The completeness of the capture is both a military report and a theological statement: YHWH's promised land grant included Bashan's sixty cities; none fell outside the reach of YHWH's commitment.
5: kol-eleh arim betsarot homah gevohah delatayyim u-veriah: 'All these were cities fortified with high walls, gates, and bars.' The emphasis on formidable fortifications (betsarot homah gevohah, fortified with high walls) underlines the supernatural quality of Israel's victory. The very obstacles that had terrified the first generation (Numbers 13:28: 'cities fortified and very large') proved no barrier when YHWH gave the battle. This contrast is Deuteronomy's implicit argument: the tragedy of Kadesh-barnea (Deuteronomy 1:19-46) was not military impossibility but faithlessness — and Og's sixty fortified cities prove it.
6: vannaharem otam ka'asher asinu le-Sihon melekh Heshbon haharem kol-ir metim uhanashim vehataf: 'We devoted them to destruction, as we had done to Sihon king of Heshbon — every city with men, women, and children.' Haharem (the herem, devotion to destruction) is the theological category that distinguishes holy war from ordinary military conquest. Under herem, persons and property are entirely removed from human use and given over to divine judgment. The parallel with Sihon's herem establishes a consistent framework: both kings received the same treatment, confirming YHWH's sovereign application of his own law of holy war.
7: vekhol-habehemah ushlal heharim bazzanu lanu: 'But all the livestock and the plunder from the cities we kept for ourselves.' The exception clause — livestock and plunder exempted from herem — follows the pattern already established for Sihon (Deuteronomy 2:35). The exemption is not a compromise of the herem principle but its authorized application: YHWH's herem targeted the human population as bearers of Canaanite idolatry; the animals and material goods could be claimed as the spoils that YHWH distributed to his fighting people.
8: vanniqqach baat hahi et-ha'aretz miyad shney malkhey haEmori asher be'ever hayarden: 'At that time we took the land from the two Amorite kings east of the Jordan.' The summary phrase baat hahi (at that time) closes the conquest narrative of Sihon and Og and opens the inheritance distribution. Shney malkhey haEmori (the two Amorite kings) — the dual victory is now framed as a unit: the entire Transjordanian territory, from the Arnon gorge in the south to Hermon in the north, has been secured under YHWH's grant.
9: Hermon yiqre'u lo haZidoniim Siryon vehaEmori yiqre'u lo Senir: '(Hermon is called Sirion by the Sidonians and Senir by the Amorites.)' This parenthetical gloss preserves two non-Israelite toponyms for Mount Hermon: Phoenician Siryon and Amorite Senir. The note authenticates the narrative by acknowledging alternative contemporary place-names — a detail consistent with the narrator's access to a period when multiple ethnic groups used competing names for the same geographic landmark. Hermon's prominence as the northernmost boundary marker appears in Psalms 133:3 and Song of Solomon 4:8.
10: kol-arei hamishor vekhol-haGil'ad vekhol-haBashan ad-Salkha ve'Edrei arei mamlekhet Og baBashan: 'All the cities of the plateau, all Gilead, and all Bashan as far as Salecah and Edrei — cities of Og's kingdom.' The triple kol (all) — all the plateau cities, all of Gilead, all of Bashan — emphasizes comprehensiveness. The southern boundary (Arnon) and the eastern boundary (Salecah and Edrei) define the territory precisely. This geographic specificity is consistent with Deuteronomy's treaty-document character: borders mattered because YHWH's land grants were covenantally defined and legally precise.
12: ve'et-ha'aretz hazot yarashnu baat hahi me'Aroer asher al-nahal Arnon: 'This land that we took possession of at that time — from Aroer by the Arnon River.' The distribution to Reuben and Gad begins here. Yarashnu (we took possession) — the verb yarash (to possess, dispossess) is Deuteronomy's preferred term for Israel's relationship to Canaan; it combines military dispossession of prior inhabitants with covenantal inheritance. The Arnon River was the traditional border between Moab and Amorite territory; Deuteronomy's precise boundary description serves the legal function of clarifying which land falls under Israel's inheritance grant.
13: ve'et yeter haGil'ad vekhol-haBashan mamlekhet Og natati lahhatsi shevet haMenashe kol-hevel haArgov lekhol-haBashan hahu yiqqare eretz Refa'im: 'The rest of Gilead and all Bashan, the kingdom of Og, I gave to the half-tribe of Manasseh — the whole region of Argob. All of Bashan is called the land of the Rephaim.' The editorial note — kol-haBashan hahu yiqqare eretz Refa'im — links Bashan's giant-clan heritage to its conquest. The Rephaim occupied Canaan before Israel; their residual presence in Bashan is now entirely ended. The land given to Manasseh carries the theological freight of YHWH's complete victory over the pre-Israelite giant dynasties.
14: Yair ben-Menashe laqach et-kol-hevel haArgov ad-gevul haGeshuri vehamMa'akhatי: 'Jair the Manassite took all the region of Argob as far as the border of the Geshurites and the Maacathites.' The personal-name notation — individual Israelite leaders staking out their allocations — humanizes the distribution. Jair's renaming of sixty cities (Havot Yair, tent villages of Jair) is an act of territorial appropriation that follows ancient Near Eastern naming conventions: to name a place is to establish ownership. Numbers 32:41 preserves the same tradition.
15: ulemakhir natati et-haGil'ad: 'To Machir I gave Gilead.' The Machirite clan of Manasseh (Numbers 26:29; Deuteronomy 27:1; Joshua 17:1) receives Gilead as its tribal inheritance. Makhir in Judges 5:14 is depicted as a warrior clan ('from Machir came down commanders'), consistent with their assignment to the Transjordanian frontier. This one-line allocation formula — the briefest land-grant statement in the section — underscores that even the simplest political arrangements flow from YHWH's deliberate distribution of promised territory.
16: uleRe'uveni ulaGadi natati min-haGil'ad ve'ad-nahal Arnon: 'To the Reubenites and Gadites I gave from Gilead down to the Arnon Valley.' The Arnon (nahal Arnon) marks the southern boundary precisely. Tokhav hanahal ug'vol — the middle of the river-gorge is the border; the formula anticipates land-survey precision. The two-and-a-half tribe settlement east of the Jordan creates a theological ambiguity that appears later in Josh 22, where the eastern tribes build an altar that nearly triggers civil war — their separation from the tabernacle cult raised questions about covenant identity that the text never fully resolves.
17: ve'haAravah vehaYarden gevul veKineret ve'ad yam haAravah yam-hamelah: 'The Arabah also, with the Jordan as the western boundary, from Chinnereth down to the Sea of the Arabah, the Salt Sea.' The Jordan (haYarden gevul) functions here as a political boundary: the Transjordanian tribes are bounded to the west by the river. The yam-hamelah (Salt Sea, = Dead Sea) and yam haKineret (Chinnereth, = Sea of Galilee) frame the Jordan Rift Valley. This geographic precision is the Deuteronomic land-grant document's way of establishing covenantal title to specific territory.
18: vatetsav etchem baat hahi lemor YHWH Eloheichem natan lachem et-ha'aretz hazot lerishetah: 'At that time I commanded you: YHWH your God has given you this land to possess.' The command to the Transjordanian fighting men — that they must cross over and help their brothers — has covenantal logic: the entire people stands together before YHWH. Halutz lifnei acheichem (armed before your brothers) — the root halatz means to be equipped, drawn out for battle. The obligation of the eastern tribes to participate in the western conquest maintains the unity of Israel as a single covenant people even while settled on opposite sides of the river.
19: raq nesheikhhem vetapchem umiqnekhem yadata ki miqneh rav lachem yeshvu beare'ichem asher natati lachem: 'But your wives, your little ones, and your livestock may remain in your cities.' The exemption of non-combatants (nashim, taf) follows standard ancient Near Eastern military practice for settlements whose territory is already secured. The parenthetical yadata ki miqneh rav lachem (I know you have much livestock) is a pastoral aside that acknowledges economic realities without abandoning the military obligation — a small humanizing detail that characterizes Deuteronomy's tone throughout.
20: ad asher-yaniach YHWH laacheichem kakem veyyarshu gam-hem et-ha'aretz asher YHWH Eloheichem noten lahem: 'Until the LORD gives rest to your fellow Israelites as he has given rest to you.' Yaniah — the root nuah (to give rest, to settle) is Deuteronomy's covenantal rest vocabulary. The same concept drives the Sabbath command (Deuteronomy 5:14), the land-Sabbath laws, and ultimately the NT's 'Sabbath-rest for the people of God' in Hebrews 4:1-11, where Joshua's rest is explicitly said to have been incomplete, pointing forward to the definitive rest Christ provides.
21: ve'et-Yehoshua tziviti baat hahi lemor einekha haro'ot et kol-asher asah YHWH Eloheichem lishnei hamlakhim ha'eleh: 'At that time I commissioned Joshua: Your eyes have seen everything YHWH your God has done to these two kings.' The commissioning of Joshua here (anticipating the fuller account in Deuteronomy 31:7-8, 14-15, 23) is grounded in empirical witness: Joshua has seen YHWH's pattern of action. Einekha haro'ot (your eyes — the ones seeing) — the participial form emphasizes ongoing perception. Joshua's authority rests not merely on Moses's appointment but on his personal knowledge of YHWH's track record: evidence-based faith in a God who acts consistently.
23: va'ethanan el-YHWH baat hahi lemor: 'And I pleaded with the LORD at that time, saying.' Va'ethanan — the Hithpael of hanan (to be gracious) means to plead for grace, to make supplication. The same root gives the name Yochanan (John: YHWH is gracious). Moses's prayer is the paradigmatic act of a leader who trusts YHWH's grace even when facing judgment. The verb implies that Moses acknowledged YHWH's right to refuse — this is not complaint or demand but petition from a servant to a sovereign who alone can show mercy.
24: Adonai YHWH Atah hahilota lehavot et-avdecha et-gadlecha ve'et-yadecha hahazaqah: 'O Lord GOD, you have only begun to show your servant your greatness and your mighty hand.' The double divine name Adonai YHWH (Lord GOD) is distinctive — more emphatic than simply YHWH. Hahilota (you have begun) — the root halal in Hiphil means to begin, to initiate. Moses's prayer acknowledges that the Transjordanian victories were only a beginning of what YHWH could do. Mi El bashamayim uvaaretz asher yaaseh kema'asekha vekig'vurotekha (What god in heaven or on earth can do works and mighty acts like yours?) — the rhetorical question asserts YHWH's incomparability, a motif that runs from Exodus 15:11 through Second Isaiah to the NT doxologies.
25: e'brah-na ve'er'eh et-ha'aretz hatovah asher me'ever haYarden hahhar hatov hazeh vehaLevanon: 'Please let me cross over and see the good land beyond the Jordan — that good hill country and Lebanon.' Na — the particle of entreaty; Moses is asking, not demanding. Ha'aretz hatovah (the good land) — the superlative adjective tov appears repeatedly in Deuteronomy as a comprehensive term for the land's desirability (the land flowing with milk and honey, abundant with every good thing). Moses's desire to see the land is poignant: he has led Israel toward it for forty years but will not enter. The refusal creates a typological pattern: Moses, the law-giver, could not bring Israel in — only Joshua (= Jesus) accomplishes that (Hebrews 4:8).
27: aleh rosh haPisga usha einekha yamah vetzaphona veTeymana umizraha ure'eh be'einekha ki-lo ta'avor et-haYarden hazeh: 'Go up to the top of Pisgah and look westward, northward, southward, and eastward. Look at it carefully, for you shall not cross this Jordan.' The four-directional panorama from Pisgah is YHWH's mercy within judgment: Moses is denied the crossing but given the sight. Ure'eh be'einekha (look with your eyes) — YHWH grants the seeing while withholding the entering. Pisgah reappears in Deuteronomy 34:1-4 for Moses's final panoramic view of the land before his death on Mount Nebo — the vision that substitutes for the possession.
28: vetzav et-Yehoshua vechazzeqehu ve'amtzehu ki-hu ya'avor lifnei ham-am hazeh vehu yanial otam et-ha'aretz asher tir'eh: 'Commission Joshua, and encourage him and strengthen him, for he shall cross over at the head of this people, and he shall give them possession of the land that you will see.' Chazzeq ve'ametz (strengthen and make firm) — the paired verbs of empowerment form a standard commissioning formula that reappears in Deuteronomy 31:6,7,23 and Joshua 1:6,7,9. These words echo through the NT: Paul's 'be strong in the Lord' (Ephesians 6:10) and the closing charge of Revelation (Revelation 3:11) draw on the same commissioning vocabulary. Joshua inheriting Moses's task anticipates the NT pattern of the one greater than Moses completing what the law could only begin.
29: vaneshev baggerem mul Beit-Pe'or: 'So we remained in the valley opposite Beth-peor.' This final geographic note anchors the narrative at the site of Israel's most recent apostasy — Baal-peor (Num 25), where 24,000 died for sexual immorality with Moabite women and worship of Baal. Deuteronomy begins its speeches from this location (Deuteronomy 1:5): the teaching is delivered precisely where Israel's failure was freshest. The choice of Beth-peor as the setting for the covenant renewal sermons is theologically deliberate — YHWH's instructions are given at the site of sin, not despite the failure but through it, as the necessary context for covenant renewal.
